title: Praxis Teaching Reading: Elementary (5205) - 200 Source Questions + 300 Guided Review
description: Contains 200 original source questions, 0 assistant-authored independent practice questions, and 300 source-derived guided-review activities. The assistant audit found 100 distinct independent content kernels. Two 100-item diagnostics, a 90-item selected-response simulation, three constructed-response task families, native learning resources, and detailed feedback for beginning K–6 reading teachers.
contentReview: Assistant audit completed: 200 independent-practice items contain 100 distinct content kernels and 100 parallel variants under the normalized test. 300 additional activities are guided reasoning transformations, not independent exam questions. The 500-distinct-question target is not met.
simulationNote: The official test provides 150 minutes for 90 selected-response and 3 constructed-response questions. This 120-minute selected-response simulation uses the exact 14/18/21/21/16 category counts and reserves three separate 10-minute workshop tasks for emergent literacy, independent literacy, and diverse learners. AlloFlow does not score written responses.
disclaimer: Independent preparation material, not affiliated with or endorsed by ETS, ILA, IDA, or IES. These original questions, workshop self-checks, and results are not official forms, constructed-response scores, scaled scores, pass predictions, licenses, dyslexia diagnoses, disability or accommodation decisions, intervention placements, or substitutes for current state, program, testing, accessibility, privacy, and educational requirements.

domains.label: Phonological and Phonemic Awareness and Emergent Literacy
Comprehension of Literary and Informational Text
Assessment and Instructional Decision Making (constructed response)

items.prompt: In a K–2 literacy lesson, how should an elementary literacy teacher use a developmental literacy continuum?
choices: Use the continuum to anticipate relationships and prerequisites while assessing the individual learner rather than treating stages as fixed gates.
Require every learner to master skills at the same age.
Skip foundational evidence when comprehension is weak.
Use grade placement as the only indicator of instructional need.
items.rationale: Literacy components develop in related and partly overlapping ways; a continuum organizes assessment and instruction but does not erase individual, linguistic, cultural, disability, or opportunity differences. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.
choiceRationales: Correct. Literacy components develop in related and partly overlapping ways; a continuum organizes assessment and instruction but does not erase individual, linguistic, cultural, disability, or opportunity differences. Have students memorize definitions only.

items.prompt: During elementary writing instruction connected to reading, how are reading and writing instruction related?

Reading and writing share no underlying knowledge.

Composition should begin only after reading is mastered.

items.prompt: In a K–2 literacy lesson, which relationship is central to reading comprehension?

Word recognition alone guarantees text understanding.

items.prompt: During systematic K–6 phonics instruction, why use decodable text during early phonics application?

items.prompt: During systematic K–6 phonics instruction, how should irregular high-frequency words be taught?
choices: Treat every letter as arbitrary.
Require visual memorization without sound analysis.
Map the regular phoneme–grapheme parts explicitly and identify the unexpected part while connecting pronunciation and meaning.
Avoid the word until every exception is taught.

items.prompt: While teaching comprehension with a grade-level text, a classroom teacher invites a colleague to observe a comprehension lesson. What should the teacher establish before the visit?

items.prompt: In a K–6 literary or informational text lesson, after learning a new text-structure routine, what action best helps a classroom teacher determine whether it improves comprehension?
choices: Use the routine unchanged for the full year before examining any student evidence.

items.prompt: When adapting comprehension instruction from evidence, a classroom teacher wants to share a student reading profile with staff members. What should the teacher do first?
